title: The Invisible Labor Behind Building a Pilates Empire: Paige Johnson's Story
guestBio: Paige Johnson is the co-owner of Mantra Pilates and a former competitive gymnast who transitioned into beloved gymnastics coaching. As a devoted mother and compassionate leader, she's known for her powerful presence and ability to build community through movement while navigating the invisible work of running a business and raising a family.
excerpt: Former gymnast turned Pilates studio co-owner Paige Johnson reveals the unseen labor behind building a fitness empire while raising a daughter. She shares how movement becomes emotional leadership and why women are consistently underestimated in business and fitness spaces.
metaDescription: Pilates studio owner Paige Johnson discusses invisible labor, female leadership in fitness, and building community through movement on The Manage Her podcast.
keyTakeaways: The invisible labor of running a business extends far beyond what clients and customers see on the surface.
Women in fitness and business spaces are often underestimated, but this can become a source of strength and motivation.
Movement isn't just about physical fitness—it's a form of emotional and energetic leadership that impacts every area of life.
Building community through shared experiences creates deeper connections than traditional business networking.
Technology tools can help busy entrepreneurs stay organized and connected while managing multiple responsibilities.
Finding your personal mantra helps you stay grounded when juggling business ownership and motherhood.
Sometimes leadership means knowing when to rise up and when to let go with grace.

pullQuotes.text: Movement isn't just fitness — it's emotional and energetic leadership
It's about the strength it takes to hold it all, and the grace it takes to let some of it go

timestamps.label: The Invisible Labor of Business Ownership
Being Underestimated as a Woman in Fitness
Closing Thoughts and Where to Connect

content:
What does it really take to build a thriving Pilates studio while raising a family? If you've ever wondered about the invisible work behind successful women-owned businesses, this conversation with Paige Johnson will give you all the behind-the-scenes insight you've been craving.

Paige Johnson, co-owner of Mantra Pilates, brings a unique perspective to entrepreneurship that's rooted in movement, community, and authentic leadership. Her journey from competitive gymnast to beloved gymnastics coach to Pilates studio owner illustrates how our paths often weave together in unexpected ways, creating something more powerful than we initially imagined.

The Invisible Architecture of Success

When you walk into a beautifully run Pilates studio, you see the polished result—the seamless classes, the welcoming atmosphere, the sense of community that makes you want to keep coming back. What you don't see is the invisible labor that creates that experience. Paige opens up about this hidden work that keeps both studios and families thriving, from the early morning planning sessions to the late-night problem-solving that happens long after the last client leaves.

This invisible labor extends far beyond scheduling classes and managing instructors. It's about creating an environment where people feel seen, supported, and empowered to show up as their best selves. It's about anticipating needs before they're expressed and building systems that allow both team members and clients to flourish. This kind of leadership requires emotional intelligence, strategic thinking, and an incredible amount of behind-the-scenes work that often goes unrecognized.

Navigating Underestimation as Fuel for Growth

One of the most powerful themes in this conversation is how women are consistently underestimated in both business and fitness spaces. Rather than letting this diminish her confidence, Paige has learned to use these moments as fuel for proving what's possible when women lead with authenticity and strength.

The fitness industry, in particular, can be challenging for women who want to be taken seriously as business owners rather than just pretty faces in activewear. Paige shares how she's navigated these perceptions while building credibility and respect in her field. Her approach isn't about proving herself to doubters—it's about staying focused on the impact she wants to make and the community she's building.

This underestimation often comes from a fundamental misunderstanding of what leadership looks like when it's rooted in compassion and community-building rather than dominance and competition. Paige demonstrates that powerful leadership can be nurturing, inclusive, and strength-based all at the same time.

Movement as More Than Fitness

Perhaps the most transformative insight from this conversation is Paige's perspective on movement as emotional and energetic leadership. This isn't just about getting stronger or more flexible—it's about how the way you move through your workout translates to how you move through your life.

When you commit to showing up for yourself physically, you're practicing the same skills you need to show up as a leader in your business and family. The discipline, consistency, and self-compassion required for a sustainable movement practice are exactly the qualities that make someone an effective leader in other areas of life.

Paige's approach to Pilates isn't just about physical transformation—it's about helping people discover their own strength, both literally and metaphorically. She creates space for people to push their boundaries while honoring their limitations, a skill that translates beautifully to business leadership and parenting.

Technology and Systems for the Busy Entrepreneur

Running a successful business while being present for your family requires more than good intentions—it requires smart systems and strategic use of technology. Paige shares specific tools and approaches that help her stay organized, inspired, and connected without becoming overwhelmed by digital noise.

The key is finding technology solutions that actually simplify your life rather than adding more complexity. This means being intentional about which apps, platforms, and systems you adopt, and regularly evaluating whether they're serving your goals or just creating busy work.

Finding Your Mantra Through Motherhood

The intersection of business ownership and motherhood creates unique challenges that require deep wells of strength and flexibility. Paige's journey to finding her personal mantra—her guiding principle that keeps her grounded—offers valuable insights for any woman trying to balance multiple demanding roles.

A mantra isn't just a pretty phrase you repeat during meditation. It's a touchstone that helps you make decisions, prioritize your energy, and stay connected to your purpose when everything feels chaotic. Paige's process of discovering her mantra through the challenges and joys of motherhood provides a roadmap for other women seeking their own guiding principles.

This conversation beautifully illustrates the truth that leadership isn't about having it all figured out—it's about showing up authentically, building community, and creating space for others to discover their own strength. Paige's story reminds us that the most powerful leaders are often those who understand both when to rise up with fierce determination and when to let go with grace.

Whether you're building a business, raising children, or simply trying to lead a more intentional life, Paige's insights about invisible labor, underestimation, and movement as leadership offer practical wisdom you can apply immediately. Her journey proves that success isn't just about reaching your goals—it's about who you become and the community you build along the way.

quiz.title: What's Your Invisible Leadership Style?
quiz.description: We all lead differently, but some of our greatest strengths happen behind the scenes. Discover how you create impact in ways others might not even notice.

questions.question: When someone compliments the success of something you've created, what's your first internal reaction?

options.text: I appreciate it, but they have no idea how much work went into making it look this smooth
I'm grateful they see the value now - it took a while to prove what was possible
I'm happy they feel that way, but the real success is how it's brought people together
I'm thankful for the recognition and grounded in knowing this is just one step of the journey

questions.question: You're in a meeting where someone questions your expertise. How do you typically respond?

options.text: I let my detailed preparation and thoughtful questions demonstrate my knowledge
I stay calm and use it as motivation to show them what I'm capable of through my actions
I focus on finding common ground and building bridges rather than defending my position
I speak my truth confidently while remaining open to learning something new

questions.question: When you're feeling overwhelmed by your responsibilities, what helps you most?

options.text: Breaking everything down into systems and prioritizing what needs to happen behind the scenes
Reminding myself of how far I've come and using that momentum to push through
Connecting with my community and remembering why this work matters to the people I serve
Taking a step back to realign with my values and deciding what I can gracefully release

questions.question: What energizes you most about leadership?

options.text: Creating seamless experiences that allow others to thrive without them having to worry about the details
Proving that limitations are just starting points and showing others what's possible
Building spaces where people feel genuinely seen, supported, and connected to something bigger
Balancing vision with wisdom and helping others find their own centered approach to success

questions.question: How do you prefer to handle conflict or challenges in your work?

options.text: I address issues systematically, often solving problems before they become visible to others
I see challenges as opportunities to demonstrate resilience and grow stronger
I try to understand all perspectives and find solutions that strengthen relationships
I approach conflicts with both firmness and flexibility, knowing when to stand strong and when to adapt

questions.question: What would others say is your greatest leadership strength?

options.text: I make complex things look effortless and create environments where everything just flows
I don't give up easily and inspire others to push past what they thought was possible
I bring out the best in people and help them feel like they belong to something meaningful
I stay grounded under pressure and help others find balance between ambition and peace

results.title: The Quiet Architect
results.description: You're the master of behind-the-scenes magic, building systems and creating experiences that seem effortless to others. Your leadership style is rooted in thoughtful planning and anticipating needs before they're even expressed. While others might not always see the depth of work you do, your influence creates ripple effects that transform entire communities. This episode will validate the incredible value of your invisible labor and show you how to leverage your architectural mindset for even greater impact.

results.title: The Rising Phoenix
results.description: You've learned to transform challenges and underestimation into fuel for your fire. When others doubt your capabilities, you quietly prove them wrong through consistent action and authentic leadership. Your superpower lies in turning obstacles into opportunities and inspiring others through your resilience. You understand that sometimes the best response to being underestimated is simply showing up and doing the work. Paige's story of navigating underestimation in business will deeply resonate with your journey and give you new strategies for channeling that energy.

results.title: The Community Weaver
results.description: Your leadership shines through your ability to bring people together and create meaningful connections. You understand that real influence happens through relationships, not just transactions. You naturally see the potential in others and create spaces where people feel truly seen and supported. Your approach to leadership is collaborative and heart-centered, building something bigger than yourself through shared experiences. This episode will inspire you with fresh ideas about how movement and community can amplify your natural gift for bringing people together.

results.title: The Grounded Visionary
results.description: You balance big dreams with practical wisdom, knowing when to push forward and when to release control with grace. Your leadership style combines ambition with groundedness, allowing you to pursue your vision while staying connected to what truly matters. You've learned that sustainable success requires both rising up and letting go, and you help others find that same balance. Paige's insights about finding your personal mantra while juggling multiple responsibilities will give you powerful tools for maintaining your centered approach to leadership.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Let's have some fun. Welcome back to The Manager, where we explore the inspiring journeys of women navigating life, work, and family with strength and grace. I'm your host, Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager: Unveiling the Invisible Work, and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, now available on Amazon.

Today, I'm truly honored to introduce Paige Johnson, co-owner of Mantra Pilates, a vibrant Pilates studio in Orange County. Paige is an exceptional instructor whose energy and warmth light up every class. She's one of my personal favorites. A former competitive gymnast and current gymnastics coach, she brings a wealth of expertise to her work, all while raising her wonderful daughter. We've connected deeply over our shared passion for movement and community.

Paige, you've read chapter one of The Manager. To start, what's one invisible task you manage to keep your studio thriving and your family grounded?

**Paige:** I think for me, my invisible task would be the energy I bring no matter what I'm going through. I could have 700 things on my plate, but when you show up to class, you're expecting me to be a 10 out of 10, not a 2 out of 10. And so my invisible work would definitely be maintaining my energy level, maintaining my confidence, being able to inspire you and taking a deep breath and making you smile and making you walk out of there feeling empowered and healthier and more fit and just happy in general.

**Aimee:** You do a good job of that. I always walk out of your class and go, "Okay, I feel like I worked out." There's never a time I walk out of your class and feel like, "Oh, wow, I should probably go to the gym now. I should probably do something else." I'm actually done for the day. And maybe the week, but I love your - I mean it's the best class.

Your blend of Pilates and you've also trained as a physical therapist.

**Paige:** So that was my goal in college. I wanted to go into the physical therapy world and I started applying to schools at the end of college and doing internships and I worked in physical therapy probably four or five different ones for about 15 years would do that in the morning, coach gymnastics in the evenings and it was the goal and then life things happened. My parents got divorced, funding was short, I started to really look at everything and I was like I don't really want to start my life $200,000 in debt and I was making money coaching and I was making money in the physical therapy office. And so I just kept learning and kept using things I would learn at physical therapy and applying them to my gymnasts and their strength and conditioning and their injury prevention techniques.

**Aimee:** So you've had a very diverse career of how but always in this physical body space.

**Paige:** The body makes sense to me. Math problems do not. I would go to school and people would say, "What do you want to do?" And I could tell you what I didn't want to do for sure. I was like, "I don't want to study history. I don't want to be writing English stories. Don't want to be in math class at all." But PE class, yes, I'm in. So, basically my major was, they call it kinesiology. However, I did get to play a lot of games in college. Every semester I got to pick a new sport to play and that was really rad.

And then child development and I got to play Ring Around the Rosie and Duck Duck Goose and Red Rover. But you're learning about how a child's brain works and how movement helps. And so the human body has just always made sense to me and muscles and the connection and the mind body connection has always really spoken to me. And I lend that to my gymnastics career because you are constantly having to be so aware of your body whereas most people don't have to be aware of your body in space per se.

**Aimee:** Oh yeah. Because you're literally flying through space when you're a gymnast.

**Paige:** Yeah. And I think one of the funniest sentences that comes out of my mouth that entertains me is I say when I worked on the circus. Not many people can say that.

**Aimee:** You're actually something that you did.

**Paige:** I did. I worked for the circus. So, and I got to work with acrobats and the athletes there and help them choreograph little elements. I love the creativity part of the choreography. But I also love helping people feel safe and strong and more stable because as a gymnast, there were so many times where I was fighting through injuries where each turn hurt a little bit. And so if I can help those people be strong and peaceful in their body to the point where they are confident with what they're doing, it shows. It shows in their performance because they're not going out there apprehensive.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And they're not performing through pain.

**Paige:** Yes. Yeah. I worked with a baseball player for a while who was in the major league and he said it hurt to bat and so but that was his thing. That's why he was being paid millions of dollars to do is get up on that plate and serve up a home run. And it was painful for him. But he had so much negative association with picking up that bat that we set the bat down for six months and he didn't touch it. He got injured and so he rehabbed with a PT, met me and we started to just rehab not only his body but his brain as well.

And so we took away all the things that were really bothering him about holding the bat and swinging and rotation and we made him stable on both sides and we worked on his body as a whole versus the one thing he was good at. So much a pitcher or something like that. And so we have a lot of instability in our bodies as far as being dominant on one arm or one side. And especially for athletes because you're "Oh, I pitch left or I shoot right," whatever it is, that side gets overworked. So we took him back and he went back and had a last couple great years before he decided to retire.

**Aimee:** How cool is that?

**Paige:** I know. And I taught him a back flip.

**Aimee:** And you taught him how to back flip.

**Paige:** I did, which made him even cooler.

**Aimee:** Yes, because anyone who can backflip is cool. I wish I could. I cannot backflip, but maybe you can teach me that, too. I might be a little old to learn that new trick.

**Paige:** In the water.

**Aimee:** In the water. I can totally do in the water. In the water. I can do a back flip for sure.

**Paige:** We started in the foam pit and he thought that was so great. But I had asked him "Have you ever done it off of a diving board? Have you ever?" He was like, "No." Great. Wow. But you want to learn this and I'm responsible for your $10 million body. And he learned it. He learned it. He learned it in 3 months. He was incredible. But we just slowly progressed him and he was ready because sometimes just learning playing can be so healing too, right? So maybe he needed to tap back into how to play again.

And he was really funny about it. So when we teach gymnastics or anything, you know, what leg do you want to start with? What leg feels best? And he was like, I want to learn on both legs. So, if I taught him a cartwheel on his right, he learned it on his left. Same with handstand kickups, roundoff backs, whatever we did, we did both sides to make him symmetrical instead of being off balance and one side dominant.

**Aimee:** Wow. Yeah, because baseball is so one-sided dominant.

**Paige:** It's a one side dominant sport. You definitely see a lot of repetitive movement injuries in baseball all the time.

**Aimee:** Yeah, I've got three baseball players at home.

**Paige:** Make them stable and balanced.

**Aimee:** Okay, I love that. I'm "All right, are you taking notes, Dad?" There you go. You've had this amazing career, but as you were going through maybe when you were younger, did you ever notice any patriarchal challenges that we talked through in chapter 1, being underestimated in your journey in the studio, as a gymnast, as a mother?

**Paige:** Yes. I feel there are so many labels that we wear. My mom was a great example. So she was an aerobics instructor and she worked for a large corporation. I feel the fitness industry for so long because it was associated with weightlifting and bodybuilding and it wasn't until Jazzercise aerobics and all of that started to emerge that you saw more females being involved. A lot of the times as far as personal training is concerned they were "well I need a dude to make me strong and push me" or guys underestimate what I can do for them because they're already very strong and so then I have to humble them just a little bit.

But my mom entered as an aerobics instructor and she was a warrior and she worked hard and she really taught me the benefits of hard work and being in an industry where females were just brand new on the scene. I think that was a great lesson for me and a good example of my mom being the one who worked, ran a company, got all three girls to all of our separate activities and made sure that all of us were fed and hair done and ready to go. So I think me moving forward, it didn't seem that impossible.

I think I get judged by one, my blonde curly hair and my bubbly personality. I don't come off as educated and having all this knowledge in my brain and until you sit down and really dig in you're "oh my gosh that girl knows some stuff" but I love to be more fun-loving and telling the jokes and that's more me than per se the very smart and let me give you your anatomy lesson.

**Aimee:** Honestly that's what makes your classes so much fun is that you're you when you - you can only be funny if you're smart and you are very funny. Very funny. I mean, and you keep us laughing. And I think that's really one of the good distractions in your class, because your class is really, really hard.

**Paige:** Yes.

**Aimee:** And so when men come into your class, what do you find they're expecting?

**Paige:** So, it depends on their male ego. And I have had it across the board. For example, getting Lawrence in there the first time, he was terrified. He did not want to be embarrassed. He did not want to be looked at. So it took me a while to convince him that you can do this.

Or on the opposite end of the spectrum, they come in with a very large ego and I don't know if the story sounds bad, but I did have one young gentleman come in who was a bodybuilder and he let me know that bodybuilding, Pilates, two separate exercises. If I want an apple, I'm not going to go eat potato chips. Two different things. And so he came in and wasn't following instructions. He wasn't doing the appropriate tension on the spring, trying to do more, more, more, more. And I finally had to say to him, I understand that you're a bodybuilder. Because he would argue with me. I know what I'm doing. I'm a bodybuilder. Just let me put the springs on. And I said, but we're in a Pilates class, so we're here to do Pilates and I need you to listen to me.

And so that's when I just made him feel a little bit. And so all the ladies in the class understood the assignment and I put them down on one yellow where you're having to use balance and stability and no brute strength. And men often don't have as good balance as females do but not all. You can't stereotype them. But I got all the ladies down in a very light balancing lunge and I said, "Everybody hold here." And he was shaking and he started to come up. I said, "No, no, we're holding. Do you see the ladies? They're not coming up. You can do this. You're a bodybuilder." And I just spoke very calmly and he was "I understand now. I understand." I had to humble him a little bit and bring him down because I think he underestimated a the type of workout, b being a female instructor who maybe even because I'm older than him was "Oh god, ladies." And not realizing that in sometimes in fitness less is more.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It's harder to go slower than it is to go faster in Pilates.

**Paige:** Correct. Yeah. And it's harder to have less spring tension than it is to have more spring tension for certain exercises.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. And understanding the difference. And so I think yes, you get kind of underestimated based on I own a Pilates studio and a lot of men will be "that's so cute. You're having a studio. How sweet. How cute is that?" So cute.

**Paige:** You're "Oh, I make grown women sweat and little tears come down their eyes." And men too.

**Aimee:** And men too, for sure. But as we were talking about with the baseball player, I kept telling him, "Come, come take a class. Come take a class." And we finally did. I convinced him to come down and he brought his wife down and we worked out and he said, "This is exactly what I need. This is exactly it." But it took some convincing for him to be "Oh, I've been on a reformer. I understand." But when I take that knowledge from the PT office, from my Pilates certification, from all my other certifications and combine them into one very powerful movement that is very meaningful and very time efficient, it speaks for itself, I feel.

**Aimee:** Yeah. No, and I think that's one of the things I love the most about your class is how efficient your workout is. Because I used to go to yoga five or six times a week and I'm actually stronger now and I only go to your class once or twice a week which is - that's saying I mean that's a lot of time saved for me and at this point in my life time saved is really really valuable.

**Paige:** It's money maybe it's money in the bank.

**Aimee:** Yes. Yeah, for sure. Chapter one highlights how technology can lighten our load. What's a tool or app you rely on to manage the demands of running Mantra Pilates?

**Paige:** My phone, my phone and our software company that we utilize for our booking system and all the things. It does it all for me. But I write my routines on my phone. I answer emails on my phone. I check classes on my phone. I log on to our app, things that. We do have an app for our studio itself. But that being said, our software company and our software provider that does all the class management and everything for us is super key. But having that phone in my hand, it's my little personal computer. And so that has really saved me from being tied to a desk all the time where I can still be out and about and still doing things and still getting work done without being tied to a chair.

**Aimee:** Oh yeah. Phones are the best.

**Paige:** They are the best. They're the best.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Or the worst. I know. But because you can work 24/7 if you want to.

**Paige:** Now, right terrifying because that's me.

**Aimee:** Yeah. In my head, I'm constantly working.

**Paige:** Yeah, totally. There's a constant list running there. I try to shut my brain down at night, but I start thinking about the routine and what I want to give to you guys the night before. And then I wake up writing routines at 1:00 a.m. And I'm "No, you don't need to write your routine right now. Go back to sleep."

**Aimee:** Yeah, totally. I actually keep a little pad of paper next to my bed in case I really just want to get up and write it down so I can actually go back to sleep.

**Paige:** Yeah. Just in case. Yeah. And it's the other list, too. What am I doing today? What's on my calendar? What am I wearing? How am I going to match? Right. Right. All the things run through my head constantly and I always joke because I am a terrible sleeper, but I am very efficient in the middle of the night of getting things done and solving world problems. So if people just asked, I have all the answers.

**Aimee:** They just kind of come to you in the middle of the night. No big deal.

**Paige:** In the morning, it's the best.

**Aimee:** So, being in the studio, lots of crazy things happen. It's a fishbowl. It's on a corner and we've got glass all around us. So, I feel a lot of times the world is watching us, but we're also watching the world. What's one of the craziest things that you've experienced at Mantra?

**Paige:** There's so many. Everybody comes in with their own personal story, their own and I have to keep that in mind. And so I don't - I mean there's so many things from things that happen in the parking lot to as we were talking about earlier watching a lady go into labor in the parking lot and have to get rushed away and people tripping and falling right in front of the studio. The kids walking by and watching, the high school kids who go to their classes right there and the interaction with each human as their own entity throughout the day because as we were talking about everybody comes in with their own little set of baggage from the day or excited to see or share, excited to work out, not excited to work out. I get all of it and then to bring it into one space and create a community feeling in that space when you have people from all different walks of life and political views and yet somehow when we're in that studio, everybody is just working out and doing Pilates.

**Aimee:** Yeah, it really is such a community and we're all together and we're all laughing at your jokes and we're jamming out to your jams.

**Paige:** And when you guys get into a routine where you're taking classes at the same time, you become a community. And I think my 5:30 a.m. class because they see each other so often and it's early in the morning. I have a group of ladies who they call themselves Pilates and Pinot. And they have become really good friends outside of the studio and I don't think they would have ever in the outside world been friends other than their connection inside the studio. And so to see those friendships grow and develop based on a product that I'm serving is pretty cool.

**Aimee:** That is amazing. Yeah, because it's kind of being in the military together, honestly. I mean, your class is boot camp.

**Paige:** I equate it to gymnastics. Your gymnastics team is your own little bubble and in one of your previous podcasts, you had talked about you didn't have a sorority. You didn't - you weren't in a sorority, and neither was I. My gymnastics team was my sorority all the way through my life. You found your people there and who you really related to and I think it speaks - my daughter did gymnastics too and watching her still have these friendships from when she was 5, 6 years old and keeping up with these girls and having that as a little sense of community. And she would say, "My friends at school just don't get it, because I'm always at gym and soccer practice is only an hour, but gymnastics is four and so they don't get that they don't get how hard gymnastics is and how much time it is, but she had her people at gymnastics.

**Aimee:** That's right. Athletics is another way to build community. It's another way for us to build community as women and as men. And if people are looking to make friends, maybe look at joining some kind of gym if you're looking to make friends, create a routine around your exercise and you will meet people.

**Paige:** For sure. Definitely. And it's your happy place. It's different than going out and meeting somebody in a bar under a situation where you may not be yourself, right, with less inhibitions. So, but when you're bonding over the grit and the pain that you're going through in class, that momentary pain that then turns into just an endorphin rush later.

**Aimee:** Exactly. Yeah. I love that. And I love that angle on building community through athletics. I'd never really seen it through that lens. I love that.

**Paige:** Well, and then just meeting you and meeting Kathy and meeting all of these people that have taken my life down different paths where you get different opportunities because you're in this same space together. And so, and meeting all of these amazing women who have forged their own paths and have worked really hard and been the best at what they do. And it just - it's inspiring and I love to dig deep because I could be that instructor that just teaches the class and lets them walk out the door. But I always want to get to know people and what they do. And so I do - I ask a lot of questions. What do you do for work? How many kids do you have? How do you do that? I ask a lot of questions because you are a part of my community and you do - it's not just about the monetary investment you're making in my studio. It's about feeling special when you walk in that door and being part of something that's a little bit bigger than yourself.

And it really - it shows. It really does. I think that's why Mantra made it. We went through COVID and you guys came out even stronger.

**Aimee:** I think so. Yeah. I think because we had to pivot so hard. And I was telling you before I went into single mom panic mode. I was raising my kid by myself. She was 14, 15, 14, turning 15. We closed on St. Patrick's Day and I was "this - this is no, this is not real." I had just invested. I had just signed the paperwork and here I'm no longer going to be making money. And there were so many unknowns.

And I called Kathy and I said, I'm going to head to the studio and I'm going to do what they were already doing in New York. I said, I'm going to do an Instagram live. And she goes, well, Paige, not everybody has machines at home. And I said, no, no. I said, I do mat Pilates. I have other - I have other talents, not just on the machine. So, I'm going to do a workout. And she was okay. So, I called our IT guy and I said, "What do I do?" And so, he put us on our studios Instagram live and then I also co-recorded it on mine.

And then from there, we both were "You know what? We could transfer all of our content onto the floor so that they can still have a workout that they can stream into their living room with things they have at home." That's exactly what I did. And it started just me being in that studio and I was filming four or five workouts a day. And then we got some of the girls from Florida involved and then we got some of the Orange County girls involved and everybody got really creative and I thought it was really cool - do you remember you couldn't order weights? You couldn't get them anywhere.

**Aimee:** No, you couldn't because everybody wanted to have a home gym all of a sudden, right?

**Paige:** And so we did wine bottles. I did a full workout holding wine bottles and here's your weights because everybody was also drinking wine at the time.

**Aimee:** That's true. There was a lot of wine drinking so it was very convenient.

**Paige:** Yes. Yeah. So we just - we got creative and we had so many generous members who still paid their memberships and said, I'm going to help see you guys through this. So yeah, it was - it was a pivot and a learning lesson, but I was so afraid of I'm 47 years old at this point and I'm going to have to go live with my mom because I won't be able to pay rent. I'd make her sit at the desk and open her computer. **Paige:** Yeah. So you want to have the knowledge because you are operating on a machine where it requires balance. It requires strength. And so it does - it takes a lot more than just a quick weekend course to become a good instructor. **Aimee:** The best of the best. Rising up for me is also about showing up and not just showing up, but giving it your 100%. I think one of those things that we talked about, the invisible moments is when clients walk in and I've got somebody who's got an injury and I have to tend to that or somebody who's brand new and then I have the person who has 3,000 classes under their belt and I have to make a class suitable for everybody in order to feel that.

And so you go back to rising up is doing those invisible tasks, showing up, being on point, constantly setting goals for yourself so that where you're at is never status quo and safe. You want to continue to grow and build and just do better. And I think that's one of the key terms that can be taken incorrectly. But as a gymnastics coach, that was one of the things that I instilled in my gymnasts is you can always do better. And when you say I'm trying my best, you can still do better than your best.
